topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The invitation felt like a deliberate trap. Liam smoothed down his worn denim jacket, the fabric rough beneath his fingertips. He stood in the sterile, softly lit reception area of the downtown clinic, a knot tightening in his stomach. Across from him sat Marcus, the boy who used to dismantle his sense of self with casual cruelty in the seventh grade. Marcus, now impeccably dressed in charcoal grey, offered a practiced, gentle smile. "Glad you could make it, Liam." Liam just stared at the polished floor tiles, his jaw tight. *This is a setup,* he thought, the conviction settling heavy in his chest. He’d expected a prank, maybe an elaborate joke. Not this quiet, professional air. When Marcus finally leaned forward, his voice a low, measured murmur, Liam felt a distinct urge to bolt. "So," Marcus began, his eyes holding an unnerving depth, "tell me about what you've been carrying." Liam shifted his weight, his hands clenching into fists inside his pockets. He looked at Marcus, searching for the familiar sneer, the sharp edge he remembered. It wasn't there. Instead, there was a strange, almost unsettling calm.

[story 2]

I walked into the office feeling like an intruder in a museum dedicated to my own past mistakes. Every instinct screamed that this was some elaborate, cruel performance designed to mess with me. Marcus was sitting behind a large oak desk, looking entirely too composed for someone who once thrived on making me feel small. I kept glancing at the framed diploma on the wall, trying to find a crack in the facade. "Look, Marcus," I started, my voice deliberately flat, "I don't know why I agreed to this. I thought you were just trying to get a laugh." I watched his expression flicker—a momentary pause, a barely perceptible tightening around his mouth before it smoothed back into that infuriatingly serene mask. My palms began to sweat, a clammy film spreading across my skin. I crossed my arms tightly over my chest, trying to project an air of hardened indifference, but inside, a frantic buzzing started in my ears. *He’s playing a role,* I told myself, though the silence stretching between us felt too heavy, too real for mere acting.

[story 3]

The first session was excruciatingly awkward. I sat rigidly on the edge of the leather couch, my gaze fixed on the intricate pattern of the rug. Marcus didn't rush me. He took a slow sip of water, his attention focused entirely on me, a level of focused observation I hadn't experienced in years. "It takes a lot of courage to come in here, Liam," he said, his tone devoid of judgment, yet somehow more penetrating than any harsh reprimand I’d ever received. I felt a sudden, sharp prickle of defensiveness. My breath hitched slightly as I tried to formulate a rebuttal, but the words caught in my throat. I forced myself to meet his eyes, trying to project an image of unshakeable composure. *This is manipulation,* a small, insistent voice whispered in my mind. I watched his face, searching for the tell—the flicker of malice, the hint of the old bully—but found only an unnerving, almost empathetic stillness. It was baffling.
